narrative:
Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 49.9% chance that you would not pass—right on the edge, but enough to tip the decision against admission. This model looks at patterns from thousands of past students to gauge future bar success, focusing on things like grades, test scores, and family circumstances without considering gender or race. In your case, three key factors stood out as having the strongest sway over this prediction: your LSAT score, your first-year grade decile, and your undergraduate GPA.

Your LSAT score of 35 was the most influential, and in a positive way—it pushed the model's prediction toward expecting you to pass, more so than for many others, since successful bar takers averaged around 37. However, this good influence was not quite enough to offset the other two factors. Your first-year decile of 4 had a notable pull in the opposite direction, nudging the prediction toward failure because it sits below the average of 6 for those who passed, signaling to the model a weaker early academic foundation compared to typical admitted students.

Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 2.60 worked against you, contributing toward the failure prediction as it's lower than the 3.24 average among bar passers, highlighting a gap in prior performance that the model weighs heavily. Your middle family income aligns with over a third of successful students, and your plan to attend full-time matches nearly all of them, but these didn't carry enough weight to shift the balance. Overall, your profile stacks up closely to many applicants, but these academic markers made the model see higher risk than in accepted students, whose stronger early grades and GPAs typically lower failure odds below 50%.

To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score even higher, as it already helps your case, and focus on building a stronger academic record through targeted prep or additional coursework to improve those decile and GPA perceptions. You might also explore programs with bar passage support or gather letters emphasizing your resilience and full-time commitment. With some strategic steps, you can reshape how models like this view your potential and reapply stronger.
